moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, staking our claim to the center and immediately controlling the crucial d5 and f5 squares. This classical move develops our position by freeing both our light-squared bishop on f1 and our queen, giving us multiple development options. The e4 pawn also supports a potential d4 advance later, which would give us a powerful pawn center. Most importantly, we're following the fundamental opening principle of controlling the center with pawns before developing our pieces.

Our opponent chooses the unusual Nimzowitsch Defense, developing the knight to c6 instead of the more common responses like e5, c5, or e6. This move does develop a piece and attacks our e4 pawn, forcing us to make a decision about how to defend it. However, Black is deliberately avoiding the central pawn breaks that would contest our space advantage, which gives us opportunities to seize even more central control. The knight on c6 also blocks the c7-pawn, which can make Black's queenside development slightly awkward later in the game.

We're playing the aggressive Wheeler Gambit, a sharp and somewhat speculative attempt to disrupt Black's development and seize space on the queenside. This pawn advance attacks the knight on c6, forcing it to make an immediate decision - it can retreat to a5 or d8 (both awkward squares), capture on b4, or advance to d4. Our b4 pawn also controls the important c5 and a5 squares, potentially limiting Black's pawn breaks on the queenside. While this gambit is not theoretically sound at the highest level, it creates immediate tactical complications that can be dangerous for unprepared opponents, especially in faster time controls where Black might not remember the best defensive setup.
